title: RL on Cartridges
text:
RL on Cartridges: Freezes the base model and applies reinforcement learning directly to a small customer- or task-specific memory module, such as a learned KV-cache cartridge, so it acquires specialized agent skills without modifying the full model.
The high level idea is to apply online RLVR on top of the cartridge itself rather than the base policy. This should enable quasi-non-parametric adaption to new tasks, ability to learn from agent traces/environment feedback, and continually adapt to new tasks without forgetting.
We introduce CARL (chosen backcronym): Cartridge Adaptation through Reinforcement Learning.

title: RL on Cartridges
text:
RL on Cartridges: Freezes the base model and applies reinforcement learning directly to a small customer- or task-specific memory module, such as a learned KV-cache cartridge, so it acquires specialized agent skills without modifying the full model.
The high level idea is to apply online RLVR on top of the cartridge itself rather than the base policy. This should enable quasi-non-parametric adaption to new tasks, ability to learn from agent traces/environment feedback, and continually adapt to new tasks without forgetting.
We introduce CARL (chosen backcronym): Cartridge Adaptation through Reinforcement Learning.

title: RL on Cartridges
text:
Introduction and Motivation
We want to learn from agent traces and environment feedback, and continually adapt to new tasks.
Enterprise relevance:

Customer-specific adaption

Instead of θ → θ', you get θ + C_customer, where every enterprise has its own evolving cartridge.

Base Model (shared)

    +

Customer A cartridge
Customer B cartridge
Customer C cartridge
Cheaper RL

Base model = 70B params
Cartridge = 40M params
RL scales roughly with the number of trainable parameters and optimizer state. Updating the cartridge only is dramatically cheaper than updating the base model.

Fast continual learning

Changing a cartridge can happen every hour upon the presence of new user data.

Isolation

Updating the model for one customer doesn’t mess up the other customer
A bad RL update only damages one customer’s cartridge. It doesn’t catastrophically interfere with everyone else.

Many parallel variants during inference

Imagine OpenAI has 100,000 enterprises Each has different preferences. You cannot realistically keep 100,000 full models But you can keep 100,000 cartridges.


Goals and research contributions:

Enable non-parametric1 adaption to new tasks
Learn from environment feedback/agent traces
Learn OOD tasks from context/information
Continually adapt to new tasks without forgetting

1 By non-parametric here, we mean that the core base model policy isn’t being updated… just the cartridge itself.
Related work
Cover:

PEFT/LoRA work

A key question of concern here is whether this idea is truly different than just doing LoRA
(Asawa et al. 2025), (Asawa, Dimakis, and Zaharia 2026)

Context distillation
Cartridges (Eyuboglu et al. 2025).

SFT vs. RL analogy 2 You can think of it like SFT + RL, where ordinary cartridges is like the base model that went through SFT and then CARL is like RLVR on top of the that (mid-training vs. post-training).

Self-distillation

2 You can think of it like SFT + RL, where ordinary cartridges is like the base model

title: RL on Cartridges
text:
Research question
We’d want to test things like:

RQ1 (RL on Cartridges): can we improve on a task with just RL’ing the cartridge without modifying the base model itself?
RQ2 (OOD Generalization): can we add OOD information/context/hints into the cartridge, that doesn’t exist in the base policy, to then reinforce during RLVR? Thereby expanding support/pass@k?
RQ3: (Continual Learning): can we sequentially train on new tasks via new cartridges without degrading performance or inducing catastrophic forgetting?

Could also be good to analyze the weight space geometry/sparsity of the updates (i.e., SFT forgets, RL doesn’t)

RQ4 (Self-distillation): Cartridge OPSD: Use on-policy self-distillation to continually compile privileged experience into a trainable cartridge while keeping the base model frozen. Compare with RL optimizer.

title: Sample post
text:
Today’s leading language models contain upwards of a trillion parameters, pretrained on tens of trillions of tokens. The transformer architecture that underpins them (Vaswani et al. 2017) keeps improving with scale, as these trillions are necessary for learning and representing all the patterns in written-down human knowledge.
In contrast, post-training involves smaller datasets and generally focuses on narrower domains of knowledge.1 This post is a placeholder that demonstrates the typography and layout of the blog, including citations, footnotes, and sidenotes.

A second-level heading
Body copy is set in Iowan Old Style at 17px with a generous line height, matching the reference design. Links like this one pick up the subtle underline treatment, and inline code is rendered in red monospace.

An italic third-level heading
Lists use square bullets:

First point about something interesting
Second point with a bit more detail
Third point to round things out


Block quotes are styled with a thin left rule and a muted foreground color, exactly as on the reference site.

title: Sample post
text:
Math and code
Inline math such as e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0 renders via KaTeX, and display math works too:

\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}}\big[ -\log p_\theta(x) \big]

A fenced code block, here distilling a teacher into a student (Hinton, Vinyals, and Dean 2015):
def distill(student, teacher, batch):
    logits_s = student(batch)
    logits_t = teacher(batch).detach()
    return kl_divergence(logits_s, logits_t)

title: Self-Distillation Without Imitation: Bootstrapping Reward Models with Privileged Information
Research Interests
text:
Ideas

SDRM
SiCPO
RL on Cartridges
SDRM — Self-Distilled Reward Modeling: Uses a privileged version of the model to evaluate and compare its own on-policy outputs, then distills those judgments into a reusable reward model for RL—learning from self-evaluation rather than imitating teacher responses.
SiCPO — Self-Informed Critic Policy Optimization: Uses privileged post-outcome information to train a critic that assigns dense, step-level credit across a reasoning trajectory, identifying which intermediate decisions increased or decreased the probability of eventual success. Like an implicit value function with TD bootstrapping/Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE).
RL on Cartridges: Freezes the base model and applies reinforcement learning directly to a small customer- or task-specific memory module, such as a learned KV-cache cartridge, so it acquires specialized agent skills without modifying the full model.
